old_claim.note: Electric Motor supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because the narrowed node covers stationary resistance-heating appliances whose electrical input produces heat rather than mechanical motion.

source_shape.source_locator: Specification and claims describe a carbon resistance embedded near the iron face, electrical terminals, flexible conductors, a plug, adjustable resistance, and a safety catch; no motor or mechanically driven element forms part of the heating mechanism.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Seely's appliance heats by electrical resistance inside a stationary iron, so its operating mechanism does not use an electric motor.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The previous motor edge came from a broad appliance category that mixed heating and motion devices; narrowing the node removes the category-level reason for retaining a motor dependency.

ontology_before: Early Electrical Appliances mixed motor-driven and resistance-heating devices and therefore carried Electric Motor as a scaling dependency.
ontology_after: Early Electric Resistance-Heating Appliances is limited to devices that turn electric current directly into heat; motor-driven appliances are outside the node boundary.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: electrical_appliances_early no longer directly depends on electric_motor.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened again to include fans, vacuum cleaners, washing machines, or other motor-driven appliances.
A primary source shows an electric motor was part of Seely's 1882 flat-iron heating mechanism.
A distinct motorized attachment is added to the scoped appliance and becomes part of the node's defining mechanism.
short_reason: Resistance heating does not require mechanical motion.
